claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 4 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To begin, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is your child?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children of different ages, let's start with one child first, and then we can assess the others.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have two children, actually. My oldest, Alex, is 6 years old, and my youngest, Blake, is 4. So they're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful, thank you! Since both Alex (6) and Blake (4) are under 16, they both automatically qualify based on age. Let me continue with the assessment, which will apply to both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: The next question is about paid work. Given that Alex is 6 and Blake is 4, I think it's safe to assume neither of them is working 24 hours or more per week. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them work at all. They're just kids!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question: **Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

Given their ages (6 and 4), I'd expect the answer is no, they don't receive any such benefits - but I just want to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't get any benefits in their own names. They're too young for that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly, that makes sense. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question: **Are Alex and Blake in any apprenticeships?**

At ages 6 and 4, I'm confident the answer is no - but just confirming!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! They're far too young for apprenticeships.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely! Let me move on to questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. **Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these to qualify:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

Which of these applies to you? (For most people living in the UK, option 1 applies.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. So option 1 applies to me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's straightforward! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question: **Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You qualify as responsible if **any** of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children**
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** (at least the amount of Child Benefit) - this includes paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.
3. **You pay maintenance** for them under a court order or agreement

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives with me at home, so that covers him. Blake doesn't live with me though - he lives with his mum. I do help out a bit with Blake when I can, but it's not a lot.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is important information, so let me make sure I understand correctly.

For **Alex (6)**: You live with him, so you clearly meet the responsibility requirement. ✓

For **Blake (4)**: Since Blake lives with his mum and not with you, I need to ask a bit more about your contributions. To qualify as "responsible" for Blake, you would need to contribute **at least the amount of Child Benefit** towards his care (either in money or equivalent in kind - like food, clothes, presents, etc.).

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The current Child Benefit rate would be the minimum you'd need to contribute towards Blake's care. 

Could you tell me roughly how much you contribute towards Blake each week or month? This could include:
- Money given to his mum for his care
- Buying him food, clothes, or other necessities
- Pocket money
- Presents
- Any maintenance payments

Even if it's "not a lot" as you mentioned, it might still meet the threshold. Also, is there any formal maintenance arrangement (like a court order or written agreement) in place for Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I give his mum about £20 a week to help out with things for Blake - you know, clothes, bits and pieces for school, that sort of thing. There's no formal court order or anything like that, just what I can manage to help out with.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that detail. £20 a week is actually helpful to know.

The Child Benefit rates are approximately £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child and £16.95 for additional children (though I'd recommend checking gov.uk for the current exact rates). 

Since Blake lives with his mum, she would likely be claiming Child Benefit for him as the eldest or only child in her household. Your contribution of **£20 per week** is close to, but slightly below, the typical Child Benefit amount for an eldest child (around £25.60).

To be considered "responsible" for Blake under the Child Benefit rules, your contributions would need to be **at least equal to the Child Benefit amount**. At £20 per week, this may fall slightly short of the threshold.

Additionally, since Blake lives with his mum, **she would typically be the one eligible to claim Child Benefit for him** - the person the child lives with generally has priority for the claim.

Let me continue the assessment for **Alex**, as he clearly qualifies so far:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question about **Alex**: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example, does Alex have another parent or guardian who might already be claiming Child Benefit for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for Alex. He lives with me and I'm the one looking after him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's clear. Let me continue with the assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question: **Where does Alex currently live?**

- Is Alex in **hospital**?
- Is Alex in **local authority care**?
- Is Alex in a **foster care** arrangement?
- Or is Alex living **normally at home** with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives normally at home with me. He's not in hospital, care, or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Final question for Alex: **Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex's care fully funded by you (rather than the council contributing to his housing or upkeep)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, the council isn't paying anything. I'm supporting Alex myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That completes the assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have all the information I need to give you my assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Results

### ✅ Alex (age 6) — **ELIGIBLE**
Great news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. He meets all the criteria:
- Under 16 years old
- Lives with you
- You are responsible for him
- No one else is claiming for him
- Not in hospital, care, or fostered
- No local council support involved

### ❌ Blake (age 4) — **NOT ELIGIBLE (for you to claim)**
Unfortunately, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake lives with his mum, not with you
- Your contribution of £20 per week, while helpful, is slightly below the Child Benefit threshold (approximately £25.60/week) required to be considered "responsible" for a child who doesn't live with you
- **Blake's mum would typically be the eligible claimant** for him, as the person he lives with

---

## Next Steps for Alex

1. **Apply online** or by post using **form CH2**
2. You'll need to provide:
   - Proof of identity for yourself and Alex
   - Alex's birth certificate
3. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)
4. ⚠️ **Note**: If you or a partner earns over **£60,000**, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply — but you can still claim and receive the benefit

🔗 **Apply here**: [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), so qualifies based on age. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You (the claimant) normally live in the UK and meet residency requirements. You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered). The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 4 years old (under 16), so would qualify based on age. However, Blake does not live with you - he lives with his mum. Your contribution of £20 per week towards Blake's care falls slightly below the Child Benefit threshold (approximately £25.60 for an eldest/only child). To be considered 'responsible' for a child you don't live with, you must contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount. Additionally, since Blake lives with his mum, she would typically have priority to claim Child Benefit for him as the person the child lives with. You do not meet the responsibility requirement for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives with you and for whom you meet all the eligibility criteria. However, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake, as he lives with his mum (who would typically be the eligible claimant for him) and your contributions, while helpful, do not meet the threshold required to be considered 'responsible' for a child who doesn't live with you.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), so qualifies based on age. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You (the claimant) normally live in the UK and meet residency requirements. You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered). The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 4 years old (under 16), so would qualify based on age. However, Blake does not live with you - he lives with his mum. Your contribution of £20 per week towards Blake's care falls slightly below the Child Benefit threshold (approximately £25.60 for an eldest/only child). To be considered 'responsible' for a child you don't live with, you must contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount. Additionally, since Blake lives with his mum, she would typically have priority to claim Child Benefit for him as the person the child lives with. You do not meet the responsibility requirement for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives with you and for whom you meet all the eligibility criteria. However, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake, as he lives with his mum (who would typically be the eligible claimant for him) and your contributions, while helpful, do not meet the threshold required to be considered 'responsible' for a child who doesn't live with you.
